text: Coins have come to the games. As you explore Fairy Fox you quietly pick a few up — and now you can spend them here. One coin arms Aurora shield for a single run: your cyan guard shimmers through the whole rainbow and every block throws off a spray of coloured sparks. It's pure spectacle — your score counts exactly the same with it on or off, and all of Ward is yours at zero coins. Earning stays gentle too: reach a new stage or set a new record and Ward hands you a coin, at most a few a day, so it's a small thank-you for playing well and never a grind.

Brim joins the coin economy with a fizz. Spend a single coin and Fizz mode turns your next pour bubbly — tiny bubbles rise through the glass, and a landing in the gold throws up a soda-foam head. It's cosmetic through and through: the fill line, the rim and your score are exactly as they were, so it's there for the fun of it, never for an edge. And you earn coins just by playing well — reaching a new stage or setting a record pours you one, a few a day at most, never a chore.

The tower has learned what the best builders always suspected: a flush drop isn't the ceiling. Inside every flush window hides a razor-thin sweet spot, and a slab set flush to the pixel is a keystone — it flashes gold and pays extra on top of the perfect. Nobody tells you where the line is; you find it by building true. Set three keystones in a row and the tower punches into the jet stream: for the next few drops every point counts double, so the steadiest hand quietly becomes the greediest one. The crane also never settles anymore — the slide used to stop getting faster partway up and coast forever, and now it keeps creeping up however high you build. And past the Spire there's a stage the start screen doesn't list. Build far enough and the sky opens.

A brand-new game, and a brand-new thing to do: guard. Shards close in on a glowing core from every side, and you orbit one shield around it — point it where the threat is, with the mouse, a touch, or the arrow keys. Cover a shard as it reaches your shield and it's blocked; let one slip past where the shield isn't and it strikes the core, and three strikes end the run. That's the whole game in three seconds: face the incoming, hold the line. The shield turns only so fast, so shards from opposite sides make you choose which to save. The catch you discover by playing: block a shard on the bright point at the very centre of your shield — not just anywhere on it — and it's a parry, deflected gold, and your multiplier climbs; a loose, off-centre save still blocks but snaps the multiplier back to one. So surviving is easy and scoring asks you to meet each shard on the point. String enough parries together and the whole ring surges, doubling every point for a few seconds. And there's a stage past the ones the start screen lists, for anyone who holds out long enough to reach it.

The walls now keep a secret for the sharpest shots. Any hit counts if your shot passes within reach of a target — but every target hides a razor line through its very heart, and a shot that threads it is a dead centre: the target bursts gold and pays extra on top of the bank. Nobody tells you the line is there; you find it by shooting clean. Thread three in a row without an ordinary hit between them and your shot catches fire — the blaze — and for the next two scoring shots every point counts double, so the perfectionist's line quietly becomes the greedy one. The targets also never stop shrinking now: they used to bottom out early and coast forever, and now the angles keep getting finer the higher you score. And past Bank master there's a stage the start screen doesn't list — shoot far enough and you'll meet it.

The planet has learned to reward the truly daring. The close-pass bonus was always on the sign — swing low over the surface on your way to a target and it pays extra. But there's a razor line even lower that the sign doesn't mention: collect a target after passing close enough to all but scrape the surface and it's a kiss — a gold ring blooms where you took it, and it pays over and above the skim. String three kisses together without a timid pickup in between and the sky answers: an aurora crowns the planet, and for a few seconds every point you take is worth double — so the flight path that flirts with a crash is quietly the richest line in the game. The targets also never stop tightening now: the pickup window used to stop shrinking at Deep space and coast forever, and now it keeps creeping smaller the higher you score, so a long run always asks for a finer thread. And past Deep space there's a stage that isn't on the sign at all — fly deep enough and you'll see it.

The ink learned to reward nerve. Your own trail has always been the thing that kills you — but skim along it now, close enough to almost touch, and the bloom sparks gold: a graze, worth a point, found nowhere in the instructions. Chain a few daring grazes together and the whole field turns Iridescent — for a few seconds every mote, prism and graze is worth double, so the reckless line you'd been avoiding is quietly the richest one in the game. The run also no longer tops out: the ink used to hit a maximum speed and coast, and now it keeps creeping faster forever, so a long bloom always has a little more pressure coming. And past Cosmic bloom there's a stage that isn't on the sign — grow far enough and you'll see it eclipse.

A brand-new game, and a brand-new thing to do: remember and repeat. Four pads play a little phrase — you watch it, then echo it straight back, tapping the pads (or pressing 1–4) in the same order. Land the whole call and it grows by one pad and the next plays a shade faster; slip up and you lose one of three lives, but the phrase replays so you can find your feet again. That's the whole game in three seconds. The catch you discover by playing: the phrase is played at a tempo, and if you echo it back on the beat — not just correct, but in time — it pays more and builds a multiplier, and a run of on-beat echoes lights the pads gold and doubles every point for a few seconds. Echo at your own pace and you're always safe; echo like a musician and the score takes off. There's a stage past the ones on the sign, too, for anyone who gets deep enough to hear it.

The chamber has more to give the deeper you go. Two things you weren't told: first, a catch struck at the dead centre of the band — not just inside it, dead centre — resonates. It pays a little extra, and if you can land a few of those in a row the whole chamber lights gold and enters a Standing Wave: for a few seconds every catch is worth double. So the very precise play you'd been treating as merely safe is quietly the greedy one — string the perfect ones together and the score leaps. Second, the run no longer stops getting faster. It used to hit a top speed and coast; now the echo keeps creeping quicker forever, so mastery always meets a little more pressure. And there's one more stage past Overtone that isn't on the sign — push deep enough and you'll hear it for yourself.

The targets have learned to lead you somewhere. They used to just appear at random spots on the beam, so every run was the same shapeless hunt. Now they arrive as routes, and each one asks something different of you. A Pendulum swings you the full width, over and over. A Feint flicks the target from side to side but keeps it close — short distances, brutal braking, because the ball carries its speed straight through a catch and overshoots every time. A Creep steps you outward one target at a time, from the safe middle toward the lip, until you're braking far later than you meant to. And a Cradle is a gift: the targets appear the shortest possible hop away and always toward the fulcrum, never toward an edge — the easiest, safest points in the game. Spot one and cash it hard. Climb the stages and the beam opens up to the mean routes: The Brink parks a run of targets right against one lip, so you have to live out there and hold it, and the Tempest brings The Reel — lip-to-lip swings on the heaviest beam you've earned. The beam also never stops getting heavier now; there's no score at which it settles down.

The air has weather now. The orbs used to fall through the same dead-still room every run — so once six of them were up, that was the whole game. Not any more: a run is a sequence of currents, and they turn as you climb. A Drift slides everything sideways, so you have to tap where an orb is going, not where it is. A Gust shoves hard and short, and the rhythm you'd settled into is suddenly aimed at nothing. A Downdraft presses the air down and every catch you had timed is late. And a Thermal lifts — the orbs hang, falling lazily together, which makes it far and away the easiest air in the game and therefore the best place to let them bunch and cash a big cluster catch. Notice it and commit. Reach Zero-G and you'll meet The Vortex, which does the heavy air and the whipping push at once. Gravity itself also creeps up forever now, so a full six-orb sky no longer means you've seen the ceiling. Watch the dust: it's carried by whatever the air is doing, so you can see the weather turn before it's named.

A new game, and a new thing to do with one button: pour. A vessel arrives with a line you have to reach and a rim you mustn't cross. Hold to pour, let go to stop — except the stream doesn't stop. What you've already poured is still falling, and it lands anyway. So you can't stop where you want; you have to stop early, by exactly the amount still in the air, and then watch it come down. Too early and you're short. Too late and it spills. Land it in the gold band just under the rim and your multiplier climbs — which means the safest pour is also the worthless one. The flow keeps changing: a Slow Draw trickles in and hands you a free brim if you keep your nerve, a Narrow Neck rockets up and blows past the gold before you've blinked, and a Stutter alternates the two so no remembered rhythm can save you. Three lives. There's a tighter window hidden at the very top of the gold, and something waiting for anyone who strings enough of them together.

The wind has arrived. The slab used to slide across at the same steady pace every single time — now the crane fights the weather, and the weather changes as you climb. A long Crosswind swings each slab in hard against one edge. A Gust hurls the next few across faster than you're ready for. A Shear alternates crawling slabs with racing ones, so rhythm won't save you — you have to read each one. And when the wind drops into a Plumb Line, slabs arrive slow and near-centre: that's your window to chain flush drops and cash the streak bonus, so take it. Climb high enough and the Spire brings The Squall. A fast slab now trails a visible streak and burns brighter, so you can see the wind coming before it's named. Every tower rises differently.

A new game — and a new way to move. Anchors hang ahead of you across an endless sky: hold to throw a rope onto one, swing beneath it, and let go. Everything hinges on when you release. Your swing flings you off at whatever angle you're at, so let go near the bottom and you fly fast but flat into the ground; hang on too long and you stall out and drop short. Find the sweet spot partway up the swing — the whip — and you're launched hard and high, straight across the gap, with your multiplier climbing. Stay on the rope to pump the swing higher before a big jump. There's a tighter window hidden inside the good one, and something waiting for anyone who strings enough of them together.

The targets no longer scatter at random — they now arrive as named layouts, dealt in a different order every run. A loose Scatter, a bunched Rack you can thread for a huge bank, a Gallery standing in a row for one flat sweep, a climbing Ladder — and as you climb the stages, the mean ones come out: Pockets tucked high against the walls where only a bank reaches, and the dense late Gauntlet. Notable layouts name themselves as they arrive. Two runs no longer offer the same angles.

The game cards on the home page are tidier. Each game's description now lives behind a small "?" in the card's top-right corner — tap it for the blurb, otherwise the card stays clean and quick to scan. The mechanic tags on the cards (and the filter bar) got a fresh look too: roomier pills with a clearer, more legible style.

Polarity has a lot more hiding under the hood. The wall of instructions is gone — you'll pick it up in seconds just by playing. And the deeper you go, the more it opens up: the speed no longer flattens out the way it used to, so a long run keeps pushing you. Land a flip razor-close, not just late, and you'll notice it pays extra — string enough of those together and you'll trip something that turns the whole field gold for a while. Keep climbing and there's a stage waiting past the ones you can see. Nothing here is explained on the start screen on purpose: the fun is finding it.

Fairy Fox Games has its own little emblem now — the game-farm icon (a sprout growing from a game controller). It sits beside the title on the home page, it's the icon in your browser tab, and when you share a link the icon shows as the preview image.

Arc no longer drops target pads at random distances one after another — each run now walks a shifting sequence of named range formations, so no two plays range the same. You'll ease through a calm Drift, walk a Ladder of pads stepping out (or back) a rung at a time, whip your power near-then-far across a Bracket, settle into a Groove that dares you to dial one distance in and repeat it, press to the far edge on a Reach, and weather a dense Fusillade crescendo. Climbing the stages opens the pool: early runs stay gentle, and the demanding formations unlock and take over as you get deeper. The notable ones flash their name as they arrive.

Orbit Slingshot runs no longer scatter targets one at a time — each run now threads a shifting sequence of named target patterns, so no two plays trace the same path. You'll sweep a loose Belt, chase a bunched Cluster, chase a marching Ring around the planet, climb an outward Ladder, brave a planet-hugging Perihelion for close-pass bonuses, and get buried in a dense Swarm. Climbing the stages opens the pool: early runs stay gentle, and the daring patterns unlock and take over as you get deeper. The notable ones flash their name as they arrive.

Symmetry runs are now built from shifting cadences, so no two plays feel the same. Instead of orbs falling to one flat rule, each run strings together named patterns — a calm Mirror, a rewarding Reflection of twins, a rhythmic Cascade, a swinging Weave, a snap-decision Split, and a dense Kaleidoscope crescendo. Climbing the stages opens the pool: early runs stay gentle, and the meaner patterns unlock and take over as you get deeper. The notable ones flash their name as they arrive.
Filter the games right on the home page — tap a mechanic (Reflex, Physics, Timing…) above the grid to show just the games with that feel, or “All” to see everything again.

Browse by mechanic: a new page groups every game by how it plays — reflex, timing, physics, aim, balance, and more — so it's easy to find another game with the feel you liked. It's linked right under the games on the home page.
Getting into the games is smoother now — no more quick white flash when the page loads. Pages announce their background instantly, so you move from the hub into Fairy Fox Games with no flicker.

A brand-new game: sort the falling sparks. Each coloured spark drops down the middle — send it into the channel that matches its colour before it lands. The twist: the channels keep rearranging, so you have to read the row, not memorise a key. Route early for a rising combo, wait to be safe when it scrambles, and don't let three slip past. It speeds up and adds channels through the stages, and every run is built from shifting patterns — Shuffle, Cascade, Rush, The Churn.

No two blooms grow the same anymore: motes now arrive in shifting patterns — a sweeping Vine you chase across the field, a wide Ring you loop, a tight Thicket to thread your growing trail through, and a rare prism Spectrum greed-rush. The meaner patterns unlock as you climb the stages, so runs keep opening up the deeper you go.

New reading menu: tap the “Aa” button in the header to switch theme (light, sepia, or dark), pick an accent colour, and set the text size — the site remembers your choice, and it carries across the whole of fairyfox.io. The pages now match the main site's look, seamlessly.

Fairy Fox Games is now an AI-managed game farm: new games are planted (a fresh one every few days) and the ones already here keep growing deeper, on their own, over time. Same games you love — a clearer promise.

Every run is now a shifting sequence of target cadences — Even, Pulse, Near, Far, Climb, Scatter — and climbing the stages unlocks the trickier ones, so the rhythm of the catches keeps building and no two runs feel the same.

Every run is now built from a shifting sequence of named formations — a rhythmic Staircase, a restful Hold, a relentless Zipper, tight Bursts, and The Wall — pulled from a seeded pool, so no two runs share the same shape. The tougher ones announce themselves as you enter them.

New game: Arc. Hold to build power, release to lob a shot at a fixed 45° — the whole skill is judging the distance and dialling the power to land on the pad. Nail the bright centre for a bullseye and keep the combo alive across three lives.
Loft now nudges you when a run ends just short of your best — the honest “so close, one more” line.
New game: Symmetry. One control moves two catchers locked in a mirror; you often can't save both sides at once, so read ahead — and chase the gold twins for a bonus.
New game: Poise. Tilt a beam to balance a rolling ball and roll it over the glowing target without letting it slip off either end. It gets twitchier the longer you last.

Every game gained a run arc: named stages you climb, an ambient colour that shifts as you go, and lifetime badges that stick across runs — so no run is wasted and the collection keeps opening up the more you play.
Prism motes are now a real greed call — triple points, but they grow your trail three times as fast, so every one you eat costs you room.
The multiplier rework: land a needed flip at the last instant to grow a ×2…×9 combo; flip early to play safe and it resets to ×1. Ride the edge for a huge score, or take the steady trickle.
The collection now sorts by most-recently-updated, with each card showing when it last changed — an at-a-glance sense of what's fresh.
New game: Loft. Keep the glowing orbs aloft — tap a falling orb to bat it back up. You can only strike on the way down, and every few points another orb joins the air.
